content.full_text:
Commissioll 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Tuchnical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Jose R. Guerrero 
Project Participar 
1990 Lombard Street 
Administration 
322-5660 
San Francisco, CA 
94123 
Dear Mr. Guerrero: 
Executive/legal 
32i-5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
November 3, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our No. A-83-242 
This letter is sent to confirm our telephone conversation of 
November 1, 1983, in which you stated that you would accept 
written advice in lieu of a formal opinion from the Commission. 
You work with Project participar (the "Project"), a 
nonprofit voter research and education organization. 
One of the 
main activities of the Project is giving technical and planning 
advice on voter registration drives to community organizations. 
In the course of giving such advice, the Project has been asked 
by these community organizations whether they will incur any 
reporting obligations under the campaign finance disclosure 
provisions of the Political Reform Act by reason of their voter 
registration activities. 
The community organizations you advise 
are unincorporated nonprofit associations. 
As I indicated to you on the phone, the Commission addressed 
the question of disclosure and voter registration drives in a 
1978 opinion. 
See Opinion requested by Melinda Welsh, Staff 
Director, Valley Oak Institute for Voter Participation, 4 FPPC 
Opinions 71 (No. 78-011, November 7, 1978) (copy enclosed). 
In 
that opinion, the Commission concluded that a nonprofit 
organization would not become a political committee with the 
attendant reporting obligations under the Act for conducting 
nonpartisan voter registration drives. ll A voter registration 
drive is nonpartisan if the following four criteria are met: 
11 
I have also enclosed a copy of the Commission's 
Sobieski Opinion, 2 FPPC Opinions 73 (NO. 75-204, June 1, 1976) 
wherein the Commission concluded that a voter registration drive 
was being carried out for political purposes and therefore 
qualified the organization as a "committee" under the Act. 


Jose R. Guerrero 
November 3, 1983 
Page 2 
1. 
The program is not controlled by any candidate or 
partisan organization or committee; 
2. 
The program is designed to register voters 
irrespective of party or political views; 
3. 
Any services offered are offered to any 
organization; 
4. 
Any solicitation of funds is not directed at 
candidates or organizations of a particular political party. 
Accordingly, the organizations aided by the Project will not 
have reporting obligations under the Act so long as their voter 
registration drives are nonpartisan. 
If any questions arise in 
the future concerning the obligations of a particular 
organization, please feel free to contact this office at 
(916) 322-5901. 
DMF:plh 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, .

A J/'11 /t~ ~ 1 
~lV(J(,~J-/~ ~,~~~,J4vy~ ____ 
Dlane Ma~a ~lshburn 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


Project Pa 
cipar 
1990 Lombard Street 
San Francisco, CA 94123 
(415) 563-2100 
October 24, 1983 
Mr. Dan Stanford 
Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Dear Mr. Stanford: 
Re: Reguest for an opinion 
pursuant to Goverment 
Code 83114 
Project Participar is a non-partisan voter research and 
education organization eligible for tax exempt contributions 
as a 501 (c) (3) organization. 
One of the major purposes of 
our project is to help community organizations who are inter-
ested in conducting non-partisan voter registration drives 
with technical and long term planning advice. 
In this regard, 
the project asks the commission for a ruling as to whether an 
unincorporated association, whose sole purpose is to conduct a 
non-partisan voter registration drive must file with the 
commission? 
The unincorporated association would restrict it self to 
conducting non-partisan voter registration and get out the vote 
activities. 
The Association would be composed of members of 
community based non-profit organizations and individuals. 
It 
would solicit non deductible contributions in order to staff 
its operations. 
The Association contemplates conducting a door 
to door canvassing campaign. 
Although no particular monetary 
amount has been targeted for fund raising it is anticipated 
that it would exceed the $500 limit established by the commission 
for partisan orgnaizations. 
The project has been asked this question repeatedly by 
community organizations throughout the state. 
Your prompt 
analysis will facilitate increased voter participation for the 
up coming elections. 
If you need further information please 
contact me.

sections.question: Whether an unincorporated nonprofit association whose sole purpose is to conduct a nonpartisan voter registration drive must file with the Commission and incur reporting obligations under the campaign finance disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: Accordingly, the organizations aided by the Project will not have reporting obligations under the Act so long as their voter registration drives are nonpartisan. A voter registration drive is nonpartisan if the following four criteria are met: (1) The program is not controlled by any candidate or partisan organization or committee; (2) The program is designed to register voters irrespective of party or political views; (3) Any services offered are offered to any organization; (4) Any solicitation of funds is not directed at candidates or organizations of a particular political party.
sections.analysis:
will facilitate increased voter participation for the 
up coming elections. 
If you need further information please 
contact me.
sections.question_synthetic: Do unincorporated nonprofit associations conducting nonpartisan voter registration drives have reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Nonprofit organizations will not have reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act if their voter registration drives meet four nonpartisan criteria: lack of control by candidates or partisan organizations, registration of voters irrespective of party affiliation, nondiscriminatory service offerings, and fund solicitation not directed at particular political parties.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated October 24, 1983) and the FPPC response (dated November 3, 1983). The response cites prior opinions (1978 and 1976) to establish the four-part test for nonpartisan voter registration activities. Response was provided in written advice form rather than formal opinion per requestor's preference.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether an unincorporated nonprofit association whose sole purpose is to conduct a nonpartisan voter registration drive must file with the Commission and incur reporting obligations under the campaign finance disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Accordingly, the organizations aided by the Project will not have reporting obligations under the Act so long as their voter registration drives are nonpartisan. A voter registration drive is nonpartisan if the following four criteria are met: (1) The program is not controlled by any candidate or partisan organization or committee; (2) The program is designed to register voters irrespective of party or political views; (3) Any services offered are offered to any organization; (4) Any solicitation of funds is not directed at candidates or organizations of a particular political party.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that unincorporated nonprofit associations conducting nonpartisan voter registration drives will not become political committees with reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act, provided they meet four specified nonpartisan criteria established in prior Commission opinions.
